exercises.title: Patch Strings in Place with APPEND, GETRANGE, and SETRANGE
exercises.problemHtml:
Module 422 treated a Redis string as one opaque blob you overwrite with SET. It is really a mutable byte array, and three commands let you work on part of it without shipping the whole value across the network: APPEND adds to the end, GETRANGE reads a byte window, and SETRANGE overwrites bytes at an offset. That matters for anything you accumulate — a growing log line, a fixed-width record, a version string you patch — because rewriting a 1 MB value to change three bytes costs a full 1 MB round trip in each direction.

Using redis-cli, exercise all three:

  • SET page:home "Hello", then APPEND page:home " World". APPEND returns the new total length, 11, not the number of bytes added. Read it back and confirm STRLEN agrees.
  • APPEND page:visits "start" on a key that does not exist: a missing key behaves as an empty string, so this creates the key and returns 5.
  • Read windows of page:home with GETRANGE: 0 4 gives "Hello", -5 -1 gives "World", and 0 -1 gives the whole value. Both bounds are inclusive and negative offsets count back from the end. Then ask for 20 30, past the end, and observe an empty string rather than an error.
  • Overwrite in place: SETRANGE page:home 6 "Redis" replaces the five bytes starting at offset 6 and returns the resulting length 11. Confirm the value is now "Hello Redis".
  • Patch one byte of a version string: SET code:version "1.0.0" then SETRANGE code:version 2 "9", which returns 5 and leaves "1.9.0".
  • Write past the end of a missing key: SETRANGE pad:key 5 "end". Redis zero-pads the gap, so the key becomes 8 bytes long and GET shows five NUL bytes before end.

The scaffold lists the commands with the offsets and replacement text left blank.
exercises.inputSpec: A clean Redis database. Three string keys — page:home, page:visits, code:version, pad:key — are created and patched by the command sequence itself.
exercises.outputSpec: APPEND page:home returns (integer) 11 and GET returns "Hello World" with STRLEN 11; APPEND page:visits returns (integer) 5 and GET returns "start"; GETRANGE 0 4 returns "Hello", -5 -1 returns "World", 0 -1 returns "Hello World", and 20 30 returns ""; SETRANGE page:home 6 "Redis" returns (integer) 11 and GET returns "Hello Redis"; SETRANGE code:version 2 "9" returns (integer) 5 and GET returns "1.9.0"; SETRANGE pad:key 5 "end" returns (integer) 8, GET returns "\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00end" and STRLEN returns 8.
exercises.constraints: Do not read a value into the client, modify it there, and SET it back — every edit must be done by APPEND or SETRANGE on the server. Do not pre-create page:visits or pad:key. Offsets are byte offsets, not character offsets, and GETRANGE bounds are inclusive.

examplesJson.input: SET page:home "Hello" then APPEND page:home " World" then GET page:home then STRLEN page:home
examplesJson.explanation: APPEND returns the length of the value AFTER the append, so 11 rather than the 6 bytes that were added; STRLEN confirms it.

examplesJson.input: GETRANGE page:home -5 -1 then GETRANGE page:home 20 30
examplesJson.explanation: Negative offsets count back from the last byte and both bounds are inclusive, so -5 -1 is the final five bytes. A window entirely past the end yields an empty string, not an error.

examplesJson.input: SETRANGE pad:key 5 "end" then GET pad:key then STRLEN pad:key
examplesJson.explanation: Writing at offset 5 of a missing key grows the string to 8 bytes and fills the untouched gap with NUL bytes, which redis-cli prints escaped as \x00.

hintsJson: You never need to fetch the value to change part of it — pick the command that matches "add at the end" versus "overwrite at an offset".
APPEND key value concatenates and returns the new total length; STRLEN reports the same number.
GETRANGE key start end reads an inclusive byte window and accepts negative offsets, where -1 is the last byte.
SETRANGE key offset value overwrites bytes starting at offset and returns the resulting length; if the key is missing or shorter than the offset, Redis zero-pads the gap with \x00 bytes.

solutionCodeJson.code:
SET page:home "Hello"
APPEND page:home " World"
GET page:home
STRLEN page:home
APPEND page:visits "start"
GET page:visits
GETRANGE page:home 0 4
GETRANGE page:home -5 -1

GETRANGE page:home 20 30
SETRANGE page:home 6 "Redis"

SETRANGE code:version 2 "9"
GET code:version
SETRANGE pad:key 5 "end"
GET pad:key
STRLEN pad:key

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
All three commands share one mental model: a Redis string is a byte array with a length, and a missing key is indistinguishable from a zero-length one. That is why APPEND page:visits "start" succeeds on a key nobody created and returns 5 — it concatenated onto an implicit empty string. The return value of APPEND is the new total length, which is the single most common misreading; it is not "bytes appended", so treating it as a delta will make any accumulator you build drift immediately. Because appends are amortised O(1) — Redis over-allocates the underlying buffer so repeated growth does not recopy every time — building a value with many small APPEND calls is genuinely cheap, unlike the read-modify-SET loop it replaces.

GETRANGE is the read counterpart and follows the same inclusive, negative-index convention as LRANGE: 0 4 is five bytes, -5 -1 is the last five, and 0 -1 is the idiomatic "everything". Requesting a window past the end returns an empty string rather than erroring, which keeps parsing code free of bounds checks. SETRANGE is the write counterpart and has the one genuinely surprising behaviour in this exercise: writing at an offset beyond the current length does not fail — Redis grows the string and zero-pads the gap, so SETRANGE pad:key 5 "end" produces an 8-byte value whose first five bytes are \x00. On a fixed-width record layout that padding is exactly what you want; on a key you assumed was text it silently corrupts the value, and since the padding is invisible in most log output the bug surfaces much later. The other trap is that every offset is a byte offset, not a character offset, so slicing UTF-8 text with GETRANGE can cut a multi-byte character in half. Reserve these commands for ASCII or for binary layouts you control — which is precisely what a bitmap is, and that is the next exercise.

exercises.title: Track Daily Active Users in a Bitmap with SETBIT, BITCOUNT, and BITPOS
exercises.problemHtml:
"How many distinct users were active today" is a question a set answers correctly and expensively: one set member per user id, tens of bytes each, times millions of users, times one key per day. A bitmap answers the same question in one bit per user. There is no bitmap type in Redis — a bitmap is a string, addressed one bit at a time — so a key covering user ids 0 through 999 999 costs 125 KB regardless of how many of those users showed up.

The convention is simple: bit offset = user id, key name = the day. Using redis-cli, build one day's bitmap:

  • Mark users 5, 17, and 42 active on dau:2026-07-01 with three SETBIT calls. SETBIT returns the bit's previous value, so each of these fresh writes returns 0.
  • Read individual bits with GETBIT: user 17 is 1, user 18 is 0, and user 500 — far past any byte you ever wrote — is also 0 rather than an error.
  • Count the active users with BITCOUNT dau:2026-07-01 (3) and check the storage cost with STRLEN (6 bytes, because bit 42 lives in the sixth byte). TYPE confirms the key really is a string.
  • Undo user 17 with SETBIT dau:2026-07-01 17 0. This time the return value is 1 — the bit's old value — and BITCOUNT drops to 2.
  • Find the first set and first clear bit with BITPOS dau:2026-07-01 1 (5, the lowest still-active user id) and BITPOS dau:2026-07-01 0 (0).
  • Count a sub-range two ways: BITCOUNT dau:2026-07-01 0 0 counts byte 0, and BITCOUNT dau:2026-07-01 0 7 BIT counts bits 0–7. Both return 1, but only the BIT form lets you name an exact user-id window.

The scaffold lists the commands with the bit offsets and values left blank.
exercises.inputSpec: A clean Redis database. One key, dau:2026-07-01, is a bitmap in which bit offset N means "user N was active that day".
exercises.outputSpec: The three SETBIT calls each return (integer) 0; GETBIT returns 1 for offset 17 and 0 for offsets 18 and 500; BITCOUNT returns 3, STRLEN returns 6 and TYPE returns string; clearing bit 17 returns (integer) 1, GETBIT 17 then returns 0 and BITCOUNT returns 2; BITPOS with 1 returns 5 and BITPOS with 0 returns 0; BITCOUNT 0 0 returns 1 and BITCOUNT 0 7 BIT returns 1.
exercises.constraints: Use one key per day and one bit per user id — do not store user ids as set members or list elements. Do not create the key with SET; let SETBIT create and grow it. Do not count bits in the client; BITCOUNT must do it. Bit offsets are zero-based.

examplesJson.input: SETBIT dau:2026-07-01 5 1 then SETBIT dau:2026-07-01 17 1 then BITCOUNT dau:2026-07-01 then STRLEN dau:2026-07-01
examplesJson.explanation: SETBIT returns the bit that was there before, so a first write returns 0. Two users are active, and reaching bit 17 grew the string to 3 bytes — a bitmap is just a string.

examplesJson.input: SETBIT dau:2026-07-01 17 0 then GETBIT dau:2026-07-01 17 then BITCOUNT dau:2026-07-01
examplesJson.explanation: Clearing a set bit returns its previous value 1, which is how you can tell "this user was already counted" from "this is the first sighting" in a single command.

examplesJson.input: GETBIT dau:2026-07-01 500 then BITPOS dau:2026-07-01 1 then BITCOUNT dau:2026-07-01 0 7 BIT
examplesJson.explanation: Reading far past the stored bytes yields 0 instead of an error; BITPOS finds the lowest set bit, and the BIT suffix makes the range a bit range rather than the default byte range.

hintsJson: One bit per user means the user id is not stored anywhere — it IS the address you write to.
SETBIT key offset value sets a single bit and returns the value that bit held before the write; GETBIT reads one bit.
BITCOUNT key counts the set bits in the whole key; STRLEN shows how many bytes the highest offset forced Redis to allocate.
BITPOS key bit returns the offset of the first bit with that value. BITCOUNT takes an optional start and end, interpreted as BYTE offsets unless you add the BIT keyword.

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
The whole technique rests on one modelling decision: the user id is not stored, it is the address. Because TYPE reports string, everything you already know about strings still applies — a bitmap can expire, be dumped, be replicated — but the bit commands let you address it 1/8th of a byte at a time. The memory argument is decisive at scale: one million user ids in a set costs on the order of tens of megabytes, while dau:2026-07-01 as a bitmap costs exactly ceil(max_id / 8) bytes — 125 KB — no matter whether one user or all of them were active. STRLEN returning 6 after writing bit 42 makes the allocation rule visible: Redis grows the string only as far as the highest offset touched, and every bit in between is implicitly 0, which is also why GETBIT at offset 500 answers 0 instead of failing.

The return value of SETBIT is the detail worth memorising: it is the bit's previous value, not a success flag. That single fact turns "mark this user active" into a first-sighting detector — if SETBIT returns 0 the user had not been seen today, so you can increment a separate counter or fire a welcome event exactly once, atomically, with no read-then-write race. Reading it as an error code is the classic mistake.

For the read side, BITCOUNT is O(N) in the length of the string but N is tiny — counting 125 KB of bits is a memory-bandwidth operation measured in microseconds, and Redis uses a population-count algorithm rather than a loop over bits. Its optional range arguments default to byte offsets, which is a real trap when you are thinking in user ids: BITCOUNT key 0 0 asks about byte 0, meaning users 0–7, whereas BITCOUNT key 0 7 BIT asks about bit offsets 0–7. Same answer here, different questions in general. BITPOS completes the toolkit by finding the first bit with a given value, and it takes the same optional range with the same BYTE/BIT choice. The limitation to keep in view: this works only when ids are dense small integers. A UUID or an email address has no bit offset, so you would need a separate id-mapping counter — or, if an approximate answer suffices, the HyperLogLog covered two exercises from here.

  B --> E[String of 6 bytes with bits 5 and 42 set]

  E --> F[BITCOUNT returns number of active users]
  E --> G[BITPOS returns lowest active user id]

exercises.title: Compute Day-over-Day Retention with BITOP AND, OR, XOR, and NOT
exercises.problemHtml:
Daily bitmaps become an analytics engine once you combine them. "Who came back today after being here yesterday" is a bitwise AND; "how many distinct users did we reach over two days" is an OR; "whose status changed" is an XOR. BITOP runs these across whole keys inside Redis, so a two-day retention number over a million users is one command over 125 KB of memory instead of two large transfers and a client-side loop.

Using redis-cli, build two days and combine them. Monday's actives are users 1, 4, 7; Tuesday's are 4, 7, 9:

  • Set the six bits with SETBIT on dau:mon and dau:tue, then confirm BITCOUNT is 3 for each. Check STRLEN: dau:mon is 1 byte but dau:tue is 2, because bit 9 falls in the second byte.
  • Retention. BITOP AND dau:retained dau:mon dau:tue writes the intersection into a new key and returns the destination's length in bytes (2, the longer input). BITCOUNT dau:retained is 2 — users 4 and 7. Verify with GETBIT: offset 4 is 1, offset 1 is 0.
  • Reach. BITOP OR dau:reach dau:mon dau:tue, then BITCOUNT — 4 distinct users across both days.
  • Churn plus new. BITOP XOR dau:changed dau:mon dau:tue, then BITCOUNT — 2, namely user 1 who did not return and user 9 who arrived.
  • The dangerous one. BITOP NOT dau:notmon dau:mon takes exactly one source key and flips every bit in the stored bytes. BITCOUNT dau:notmon is 5, not "everyone who was inactive" — it only knows about the 8 bits Monday happened to occupy.

The scaffold lists the commands with the operation names and destination keys left blank.
exercises.inputSpec: A clean Redis database. Two source bitmaps, dau:mon (users 1, 4, 7) and dau:tue (users 4, 7, 9), plus four destination keys produced by BITOP.
exercises.outputSpec: The six SETBIT calls each return (integer) 0; BITCOUNT is 3 for both days; STRLEN dau:mon is 1 and STRLEN dau:tue is 2; BITOP AND returns (integer) 2 and BITCOUNT dau:retained is 2, with GETBIT offset 4 returning 1 and offset 1 returning 0; BITOP OR returns 2 and BITCOUNT dau:reach is 4; BITOP XOR returns 2 and BITCOUNT dau:changed is 2; BITOP NOT returns 1 and BITCOUNT dau:notmon is 5.
exercises.constraints: All bitwise work happens server-side — never fetch two bitmaps and combine them in the client. BITOP NOT accepts exactly one source key; every other operation accepts one or more. Destination keys must be new keys, not one of the sources. Do not use SET to preallocate any key.

examplesJson.input: dau:mon has bits 1,4,7 and dau:tue has bits 4,7,9; BITOP AND dau:retained dau:mon dau:tue then BITCOUNT dau:retained
examplesJson.explanation: The first 2 is the destination length in BYTES (as long as the longest input), the second is the retention count — users 4 and 7 were active on both days.

examplesJson.input: BITOP OR dau:reach dau:mon dau:tue then BITCOUNT dau:reach then BITOP XOR dau:changed dau:mon dau:tue then BITCOUNT dau:changed
examplesJson.explanation: OR gives distinct reach across both days: users 1, 4, 7, 9. XOR keeps only bits that differ: user 1 churned and user 9 is new.

examplesJson.input: STRLEN dau:mon then BITOP NOT dau:notmon dau:mon then BITCOUNT dau:notmon
examplesJson.explanation: Monday occupies a single byte, so NOT flips only those 8 bits: 3 were set, so 5 come back set. It is not the count of all inactive users in the system.

hintsJson: Each analytics question maps to one boolean operator over the two day-keys — decide which before typing anything.
BITOP operation destkey srckey [srckey ...] writes the result into destkey and returns that key length in bytes.
Both days plus AND gives retention; either day plus OR gives reach; exactly one day plus XOR gives churn plus new arrivals.

A shorter source is treated as zero-padded to the length of the longest, which is why AND with a 1-byte and a 2-byte key produces a 2-byte result. NOT takes exactly one source and only flips the bits that are actually stored.

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
Because every daily key uses the same bit-offset-equals-user-id convention, the keys are directly comparable and set algebra collapses into boolean algebra. AND keeps a bit only where both days had it, which is the definition of retention; OR keeps it where either did, which is distinct reach; XOR keeps it where the two differ, which merges churn and new arrivals into one number. The pattern generalises: BITOP AND weekly:retained dau:mon dau:tue dau:wed ... takes as many source keys as you like, so a seven-day "active every day" cohort is still a single command. The result lands in a real key, so it can be counted, expired with EXPIRE, or fed into the next BITOP.

Two return values must not be confused. BITOP returns the size of the destination in bytes — here 2, matching the longest input — while BITCOUNT returns the number of set bits, which is the answer you actually wanted. Reading the BITOP result as a user count is the single most common bug in this pattern, and it is silent because both are plausible small integers.

The zero-padding rule explains the byte sizes: sources of unequal length are conceptually padded with zero bytes up to the longest, so dau:mon (1 byte) AND dau:tue (2 bytes) yields a 2-byte key whose second byte is all zeros. That is exactly right for AND, OR, and XOR, because an absent bit genuinely means "not active". It is exactly wrong for NOT, and this exercise makes that visible: BITOP NOT dau:notmon dau:mon returns a 1-byte key with BITCOUNT 5, which is "the inactive users among ids 0–7", not "all inactive users". Nothing in the bitmap records how many users exist, so a meaningful complement requires you to supply that bound yourself — typically by ANDing the NOT result against a bitmap of all registered ids. Performance-wise BITOP is O(N) over the longest input and runs on the single-threaded server, so a multi-megabyte operation does block other clients; run large rollups off-peak, store the result, and read the cheap BITCOUNT afterwards.

exercises.title: Estimate Unique Visitors with HyperLogLog and Measure Its Error
exercises.problemHtml:
Bitmaps need dense integer ids. When the thing you are counting is a session token, an email, or an IP address, there is no bit offset to use — and a set of ten million such values costs hundreds of megabytes per day. A HyperLogLog answers "how many distinct values" from a fixed 12 KB sketch, whatever the values look like and however many there are, at the price of an approximate answer with a standard error around 0.81%. It cannot list its members, and that is the deal: you trade enumeration and exactness for constant memory.

Using redis-cli, count sessions and then measure the error yourself:

  • PFADD dau:hll:mon "u1" "u2" "u3" "u4" returns 1, meaning "the sketch changed", not "4 items added". PFCOUNT dau:hll:mon returns 4.
  • Re-add "u1" "u2". Nothing new is observed, so PFADD returns 0 and PFCOUNT is still 4.
  • Build dau:hll:tue from "u3" "u4" "u5", count it (3), then run PFCOUNT dau:hll:mon dau:hll:tue — passing several keys gives the cardinality of their union, 5, without creating anything.
  • Persist that union with PFMERGE dau:hll:week dau:hll:mon dau:hll:tue (replies OK) and confirm PFCOUNT dau:hll:week is 5.
  • Prove it is a string: TYPE dau:hll:mon returns string and STRLEN is only 28 bytes, because Redis uses a compact sparse encoding while the cardinality is small.
  • Measure the error. The scaffold already contains a PFADD dau:hll:big line with 200 distinct members, s1 through s200. Run it, then PFCOUNT dau:hll:big — the answer is 201, not 200. Check STRLEN dau:hll:big: 507 bytes, still sparse.

Report the number you actually get. An estimator that is off by one at 200 is behaving exactly as designed.
exercises.inputSpec: A clean Redis database. Three small HyperLogLog keys (dau:hll:mon, dau:hll:tue, dau:hll:week) plus dau:hll:big, which receives 200 distinct members s1..s200 supplied by the scaffold.
exercises.outputSpec: The first PFADD returns (integer) 1 and PFCOUNT returns 4; re-adding existing members returns (integer) 0 and PFCOUNT stays 4; dau:hll:tue counts 3; PFCOUNT over both keys returns 5; PFMERGE replies OK and PFCOUNT dau:hll:week returns 5; TYPE returns string and STRLEN dau:hll:mon returns 28; after adding 200 distinct members PFCOUNT dau:hll:big returns 201 and STRLEN dau:hll:big returns 507.
exercises.constraints: Do not use a set to cross-check the counts — the point is the sketch. Do not expect PFCOUNT to be exact: quote the value you observe. PFADD returns 0 or 1 only, never a member count. A HyperLogLog cannot be enumerated, so there is no PF equivalent of SMEMBERS. Do not use DEL between steps.

examplesJson.input: PFADD dau:hll:mon "u1" "u2" "u3" "u4" then PFCOUNT dau:hll:mon then PFADD dau:hll:mon "u1" "u2" then PFCOUNT dau:hll:mon
examplesJson.explanation: PFADD is a change flag: 1 means the internal registers were updated, 0 means every value was already represented. The estimate is unchanged by duplicates.

examplesJson.input: PFCOUNT dau:hll:mon dau:hll:tue then PFMERGE dau:hll:week dau:hll:mon dau:hll:tue then PFCOUNT dau:hll:week
examplesJson.explanation: Multi-key PFCOUNT computes the union on the fly (u1..u5 across the two days, with u3 and u4 shared); PFMERGE stores that same union in a new key for reuse.

examplesJson.input: PFADD dau:hll:big with 200 distinct members s1 through s200, then PFCOUNT dau:hll:big then STRLEN dau:hll:big
examplesJson.explanation: The true cardinality is 200 but the sketch reports 201 — a real, reproducible 0.5% overestimate. The whole structure is still only 507 bytes.

hintsJson: The requirement is a count of distinct values, not the values themselves — that permission is what buys constant memory.
PFADD key element [element ...] observes values; PFCOUNT key returns the estimated cardinality.
PFADD returns 1 if the estimate may have changed and 0 otherwise, so it is never a count of how many elements you passed.
PFCOUNT with several keys returns the cardinality of their union without writing anything; PFMERGE dest src [src ...] stores that union in dest and replies OK.

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
A HyperLogLog does not store what you add. PFADD hashes each element and keeps only the position of the longest run of leading zero bits seen in each of 16384 registers; the count is then derived from those registers. That is why the structure is bounded at roughly 12 KB in its dense form no matter whether you observe a thousand values or a billion, and why PFCOUNT can never be turned back into a member list. The keys are ordinary strings — TYPE proves it — so they expire, replicate, and dump like anything else, and STRLEN of 28 bytes for four members shows that Redis starts in a sparse encoding and only promotes to the full 12 KB dense layout once the cardinality justifies it. Even at 200 members the sketch here is still only 507 bytes.

The honest number is the point of the last step. With exactly 200 distinct members, PFCOUNT reports 201 on this Redis 7.4 build — reproducibly, because the hash is deterministic. That is a 0.5% overestimate, comfortably inside the documented ~0.81% standard error, and it is not a bug to be worked around. The practical rule: use a HyperLogLog when the consumer is a dashboard, a trend line, or a threshold check, and never when the number drives billing, quota enforcement, or anything a user will dispute. Small cardinalities are usually exact, which makes the failure mode nasty — testing with ten items proves nothing about production.

Two return values deserve care. PFADD is a change flag, returning 1 when the registers were modified and 0 when nothing new was observed, so it can never tell you how many elements you passed and must not be summed. And multi-key PFCOUNT is the sketch's best trick: because merging registers is just a per-register maximum, the union of several sketches is computed exactly as though every element had been added to one — no double counting, unlike summing separate daily counts. That gives you seven daily keys and an accurate weekly unique number for free, with PFMERGE persisting the result when the same union is read repeatedly. Note the asymmetry: unions are cheap and exact, but there is no PFINTERSECT, because intersection cardinality cannot be recovered from these registers. If you need retention — "active both days" — go back to bitmaps and BITOP AND, or keep real sets.

exercises.title: Build a Nearby-Places Lookup with GEOADD, GEOSEARCH, and GEODIST
exercises.problemHtml:
"Show me the places within 5 km, nearest first" is a query most databases answer with a spatial index and a slow scan. Redis encodes each point's latitude and longitude into a single 52-bit geohash integer and stores it as the score of a sorted set member, which turns proximity search into a handful of range scans. There is no separate geo type — TYPE on a geo key says zset — so everything you know about sorted sets still applies.

Using redis-cli, build a places index for Hanoi and query it:

  • Add four points in one GEOADD places:hanoi call, each as longitude latitude member — note the order, longitude first: 105.8542 21.0285 "hoan-kiem-lake", 105.8350 21.0378 "west-lake", 105.8412 21.0245 "temple-of-literature", and 106.6822 20.9500 "ha-long-bay". It returns 4, the number of new members. Confirm ZCARD is 4 and TYPE is zset.
  • Measure with GEODIST places:hanoi "hoan-kiem-lake" "west-lake", which returns metres as a string, then repeat with the km unit. Ask for a member that does not exist and observe (nil).
  • Read stored coordinates with GEOPOS places:hanoi "hoan-kiem-lake". The values come back with more decimal places than you supplied — geohash encoding is lossy. GEOPOS for a missing member returns a nil entry inside the array, not an error.
  • Search around a member: GEOSEARCH places:hanoi FROMMEMBER "hoan-kiem-lake" BYRADIUS 5 km ASC. Three places are within 5 km, nearest first, and the origin member is included in its own result.
  • Search around an arbitrary point with distances shown: GEOSEARCH places:hanoi FROMLONLAT 105.8542 21.0285 BYRADIUS 5 km WITHDIST ASC. Then cap the result with COUNT 2.
  • Switch the shape: GEOSEARCH places:hanoi FROMMEMBER "hoan-kiem-lake" BYBOX 200 200 km ASC uses a width-by-height rectangle instead of a circle and is wide enough to pull in Ha Long Bay.

The scaffold lists the commands with the coordinates, units, and search options left blank.
exercises.inputSpec: A clean Redis database. One geo key, places:hanoi, holding four named points given as longitude, latitude pairs.
exercises.outputSpec: GEOADD returns (integer) 4, ZCARD returns 4 and TYPE returns zset; GEODIST returns "2246.0997" in metres and "2.2461" in km, and (nil) for a member that does not exist; GEOPOS returns the stored longitude 105.85420221090316772 and latitude 21.02849918560288245, and a nil entry for a missing member; GEOSEARCH FROMMEMBER BYRADIUS 5 km ASC lists hoan-kiem-lake, temple-of-literature, west-lake; the WITHDIST variant adds distances 0.0002, 1.4212 and 2.2459; COUNT 2 truncates to the first two; BYBOX 200 200 km additionally returns ha-long-bay last.
exercises.constraints: Coordinates are longitude first, then latitude — the reverse of the usual "lat,long" spoken order. Use GEOSEARCH, not the deprecated GEORADIUS. Do not compute distances in the client. Do not assume GEOPOS returns exactly what you stored; the geohash encoding is lossy. Valid latitudes are -85.05112878 to 85.05112878.

examplesJson.input: GEOADD places:hanoi with four points, then GEODIST places:hanoi "hoan-kiem-lake" "west-lake" then GEODIST places:hanoi "hoan-kiem-lake" "west-lake" km
examplesJson.explanation: GEOADD returns the count of newly added members. GEODIST defaults to metres and returns a bulk string; adding the unit km converts server-side.

examplesJson.input: GEOSEARCH places:hanoi FROMLONLAT 105.8542 21.0285 BYRADIUS 5 km WITHDIST ASC
examplesJson.explanation: WITHDIST turns each row into a member plus distance pair, ASC sorts nearest first, and ha-long-bay is excluded because it is far outside 5 km.

examplesJson.input: GEOPOS places:hanoi "hoan-kiem-lake" then GEOPOS places:hanoi "nowhere"
examplesJson.explanation: The returned coordinates differ from the input in the far decimals because the point was encoded into a 52-bit geohash. A missing member yields a nil element rather than an error.

hintsJson: A geo key is a sorted set whose scores are geohashes, so adding a point is one command and searching is a score range scan Redis does for you.
GEOADD key longitude latitude member [longitude latitude member ...] — longitude comes first.
GEOSEARCH key FROMMEMBER member or FROMLONLAT long lat, then BYRADIUS radius unit or BYBOX width height unit, then ASC or DESC.
Add WITHDIST to get the distance beside each member, WITHCOORD for its coordinates, and COUNT n to cap the result. GEODIST key member1 member2 unit measures two stored members directly.

GEOSEARCH places:hanoi FROMLONLAT 105.8542 21.0285 BYRADIUS 5 km ASC ____ 2
GEOSEARCH places:hanoi FROMMEMBER "hoan-kiem-lake" ____ 200 200 km ASC

solutionCodeJson.name: commands.redis
solutionCodeJson.code:
GEOADD places:hanoi 105.8542 21.0285 "hoan-kiem-lake" 105.8350 21.0378 "west-lake" 105.8412 21.0245 "temple-of-literature" 106.6822 20.9500 "ha-long-bay"
ZCARD places:hanoi
TYPE places:hanoi
GEODIST places:hanoi "hoan-kiem-lake" "west-lake"
GEODIST places:hanoi "hoan-kiem-lake" "west-lake" km
GEODIST places:hanoi "hoan-kiem-lake" "nowhere"

GEOSEARCH places:hanoi FROMLONLAT 105.8542 21.0285 BYRADIUS 5 km WITHDIST ASC
GEOSEARCH places:hanoi FROMLONLAT 105.8542 21.0285 BYRADIUS 5 km ASC COUNT 2
GEOSEARCH places:hanoi FROMMEMBER "hoan-kiem-lake" BYBOX 200 200 km ASC

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
The geo commands are a thin, well-chosen layer over the sorted set. GEOADD interleaves the bits of the longitude and latitude into a single 52-bit integer — a geohash — and calls ZADD with that integer as the score. Nearby points get numerically nearby scores, so GEOSEARCH can answer a proximity query with a small number of ZRANGEBYSCORE scans over the boxes that cover the search area, then filter the candidates by exact distance. That is why TYPE reports zset, why ZCARD counts your places, and why ZREM is how you delete a point — there is no GEOREM.

Three details cause most real bugs. First, argument order is longitude then latitude, the opposite of how coordinates are spoken and of what most map UIs hand you; swapping them does not error, it silently files your Hanoi café somewhere in the ocean. Second, the encoding is lossy: GEOPOS returns 105.85420221090316772 for a point stored as 105.8542, an error under a metre that is irrelevant for "restaurants near me" and unacceptable as a system of record. Keep the authoritative coordinates in your primary database and treat the Redis key as an index. Third, GEODIST and GEOSEARCH return bulk strings, not numbers, so a client that compares them without parsing will sort "10.5" before "9.1".

On the query side, BYRADIUS takes a circle and BYBOX a width-by-height rectangle centred on the origin — the box is the right shape for a map viewport, which is also rectangular, and here 200 by 200 km is what pulls Ha Long Bay into the result that the 5 km circle excluded. FROMMEMBER searches around a stored place and always includes that member itself at distance ~0, which you usually have to filter out. FROMLONLAT searches around an arbitrary point such as the user's GPS fix. Always pair a search with ASC and COUNT n when rendering a "nearest N" list: without COUNT a dense city key can return thousands of members over the wire, and without ASC the order is not the one your UI implies. Note the cost — roughly O(N + log M) where N is the number of candidates inside the covered boxes — so a very large radius on a dense key is expensive on a single-threaded server. Finally, prefer GEOSEARCH over GEORADIUS and GEORADIUSBYMEMBER: those have been deprecated since Redis 6.2 and GEOSEARCH subsumes both.

exercises.title: Append and Read an Order Event Log with XADD, XRANGE, and XREAD
exercises.problemHtml:
A list can hold an event log, but it forces a choice you should not have to make: LPOP consumes the event so only one reader ever sees it, and LRANGE leaves you tracking positions by index, which shift the moment anything is trimmed. A stream is Redis's append-only log built for this: every entry gets a monotonically increasing id and a set of field-value pairs, entries are never consumed by being read, and any number of readers can resume from the last id they processed.

Using redis-cli, build an order event log with explicit ids so the output is reproducible:

  • Append three entries to orders:stream with XADD, giving each an explicit id instead of *: 1700000000000-1 with fields event created, order 1001, amount 250; then 1700000000000-2 with event paid for the same order; then 1700000005000-1 with event created, order 1002, amount 80. XADD returns the id it stored.
  • Confirm XLEN orders:stream is 3 and TYPE is stream.
  • Read the whole log with XRANGE orders:stream - +, where - and + are the minimum and maximum possible ids. Each entry prints as its id followed by a flat field-value array.
  • Page it: XRANGE orders:stream - + COUNT 1 returns only the first entry. Then start from a given id: XRANGE orders:stream 1700000000000-2 + returns two entries, because XRANGE bounds are inclusive.
  • Read the newest entry with XREVRANGE orders:stream + - COUNT 1 — note the reversed bound order.
  • Consume like a follower: XREAD COUNT 5 STREAMS orders:stream 1700000000000-2 returns everything strictly after that id — one entry — because XREAD takes a last-seen id, not a range. Repeat it with the id of the newest entry and observe (nil), meaning "nothing new yet".

Do not use BLOCK here; a blocking XREAD would hang a scripted session. Consumer groups are a later module. The scaffold lists the commands with the ids and range bounds blank.
exercises.inputSpec: A clean Redis database. One stream key, orders:stream, receives three entries with explicit ids 1700000000000-1, 1700000000000-2 and 1700000005000-1.
exercises.outputSpec: Each XADD echoes the id it stored as a quoted string; XLEN returns (integer) 3 and TYPE returns stream; XRANGE - + prints all three entries as id plus a nested field-value array; COUNT 1 prints only the first; XRANGE starting at 1700000000000-2 prints two entries because the bound is inclusive; XREVRANGE + - COUNT 1 prints the newest entry 1700000005000-1; XREAD after 1700000000000-2 returns the stream name wrapping the single entry 1700000005000-1; XREAD after 1700000005000-1 returns (nil).
exercises.constraints: Use explicit entry ids, not *, so the run is reproducible. Never use BLOCK in this exercise. Do not use consumer-group commands (XGROUP, XREADGROUP, XACK) — they belong to the later streams module. Do not delete entries. Entry ids must increase strictly; re-adding an id that is not greater than the last one is an error.

examplesJson.input: XADD orders:stream 1700000000000-1 event "created" order "1001" amount "250" then XLEN orders:stream then TYPE orders:stream
examplesJson.explanation: XADD returns the id under which the entry was stored, which is the explicit id here; the key is created on first append and has its own stream type.

examplesJson.input: XRANGE orders:stream 1700000000000-2 +
examplesJson.explanation: XRANGE bounds are inclusive, so the entry whose id you named is returned along with everything after it, and + stands for the highest possible id.

examplesJson.input: XREAD COUNT 5 STREAMS orders:stream 1700000005000-1
examplesJson.explanation: XREAD returns entries strictly greater than the id you pass, so passing the newest id means there is nothing new and the reply is nil rather than an empty array.

hintsJson: Reading must not destroy the event, and readers must be able to resume — that rules out LPOP and index-based paging.
XADD key id field value [field value ...] appends one entry; pass * to let Redis generate the id, or an explicit id for reproducibility.
XRANGE key start end reads an inclusive range where - is the smallest id and + the largest; COUNT limits it and XREVRANGE reads backwards with the bounds swapped.
XREAD COUNT n STREAMS key last-id returns only entries strictly after last-id, which is how a follower resumes; when there is nothing newer it returns nil.

starterCodeJson.name: commands.redis
starterCodeJson.code:
XADD orders:stream ____ event "created" order "1001" amount "250"
XADD orders:stream ____ event "paid" order "1001" amount "250"
XADD orders:stream ____ event "created" order "1002" amount "80"
XLEN orders:stream
TYPE orders:stream
XRANGE orders:stream ____ ____
XRANGE orders:stream - + ____ 1
XRANGE orders:stream ____ +
XREVRANGE orders:stream ____ ____ COUNT 1
XREAD COUNT 5 STREAMS orders:stream ____
XREAD COUNT 5 STREAMS orders:stream ____

solutionCodeJson.name: commands.redis
solutionCodeJson.code:
XADD orders:stream 1700000000000-1 event "created" order "1001" amount "250"
XADD orders:stream 1700000000000-2 event "paid" order "1001" amount "250"
XADD orders:stream 1700000005000-1 event "created" order "1002" amount "80"
XLEN orders:stream
TYPE orders:stream
XRANGE orders:stream - +
XRANGE orders:stream - + COUNT 1
XRANGE orders:stream 1700000000000-2 +
XREVRANGE orders:stream + - COUNT 1
XREAD COUNT 5 STREAMS orders:stream 1700000000000-2
XREAD COUNT 5 STREAMS orders:stream 1700000005000-1

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
The entry id is what makes a stream different from a list. It has the form milliseconds-sequence, so 1700000000000-1 and 1700000000000-2 are two events in the same millisecond distinguished by the sequence part, and 1700000005000-1 is five seconds later. Ids increase strictly and are never reused, which gives every reader a stable cursor: you record the last id you handled and ask for what came after. Compare that with a list, where LRANGE positions shift under you as soon as anything is pushed or trimmed. In production you pass * and let Redis generate the id from the server clock — explicit ids are used here only so the output is reproducible, and note that an id must be strictly greater than the stream's current last id or XADD fails.

The two read commands answer different questions and are constantly confused. XRANGE key start end is a range query with inclusive bounds, using - and + as the minimum and maximum ids — that is why starting at 1700000000000-2 returns that very entry plus everything after it. It is the right tool for auditing, backfilling, or paging through history with COUNT, taking the last id of one page as the start of the next. XREVRANGE is the same query walking backwards, which is why its bounds are written + -; reversing them by habit returns an empty result rather than an error. XREAD, by contrast, takes a last-seen id and returns only entries strictly greater than it, which is exactly the semantics a follower needs to resume without re-processing the entry it just handled. When nothing newer exists it replies (nil), distinguishable from an empty range, and its reply is nested one level deeper because a single XREAD can watch several streams at once and therefore labels each block with its key name.

Crucially, reading never removes anything: XLEN stays 3 after every query, so ten independent consumers can each replay the log from their own position — the fan-out that a list simply cannot do. The cost is that a stream grows forever unless you bound it, with XADD key MAXLEN ~ 10000 * ... being the usual idiom. The piece still missing is coordination: with plain XREAD, every consumer sees every entry and there is no record of what was acknowledged, so work cannot be divided across a pool of workers or recovered after a crash. That is what consumer groups solve, and they are the subject of the dedicated streams module.

exercises.title: Group Commands with MULTI and EXEC — and Watch a Runtime Error Not Roll Back
exercises.problemHtml:
Redis calls MULTI/EXEC a transaction, and the word imports expectations from SQL that are wrong here. What Redis actually guarantees is isolation: the queued commands run one after another with no other client's command interleaved. What it does not provide is atomicity in the rollback sense — if one queued command fails at execution time, the ones before it stay applied and the ones after it still run. Believing otherwise produces corrupted state that no error handler catches, because the failure is buried inside a reply array most clients never inspect.

Using redis-cli, walk through all four behaviours:

  • Seed account:balance with 100 and account:owner with "ann".
  • Happy path. MULTI replies OK; then INCRBY account:balance 50 and GET account:balance each reply QUEUED instead of their real answer. EXEC runs both and returns an array of the queued replies: (integer) 150 then "150". Confirm with a plain GET.
  • Abandon. Open another MULTI, queue INCRBY account:balance 10, then DISCARD. It replies OK, the queue is thrown away, and the balance is still "150".
  • Runtime error, no rollback. Queue three commands: SET account:log "started", then INCR account:owner (which will fail — "ann" is not a number), then SET account:log "finished". All three queue fine, because the error is only detectable at run time. EXEC returns an array whose second element is an error while elements one and three succeeded. Prove it: GET account:log is "finished" and GET account:owner is still "ann". Nothing was undone.
  • Queue-time error, whole transaction aborted. Open MULTI, queue SET account:tmp 1, then send NOSUCHCOMMAND arg. Redis rejects it immediately and remembers the transaction is poisoned, so EXEC returns EXECABORT and runs nothing. EXISTS account:tmp is 0.

The scaffold gives the command skeleton with the transaction keywords left blank.
exercises.inputSpec: A clean Redis database. Keys account:balance (numeric), account:owner (the non-numeric string "ann"), account:log and account:tmp are touched by four separate MULTI blocks.
exercises.outputSpec: MULTI replies OK and each queued command replies QUEUED; the first EXEC returns an array of (integer) 150 and "150", and GET account:balance returns "150"; DISCARD replies OK and GET account:balance is still "150"; the third EXEC returns a three-element array of OK, (error) ERR value is not an integer or out of range, and OK, after which GET account:log returns "finished" and GET account:owner returns "ann"; the bad command reports (error) ERR unknown command, EXEC then returns (error) EXECABORT Transaction discarded because of previous errors, and EXISTS account:tmp returns (integer) 0.
exercises.constraints: Use MULTI, EXEC and DISCARD only — no Lua, no WATCH in this exercise. Do not try to undo the failed command manually; the point is to observe that Redis does not. Do not put a blocking command inside MULTI. Every command between MULTI and EXEC must reply QUEUED for the transaction to be valid.

examplesJson.input: MULTI then INCRBY account:balance 50 then GET account:balance then EXEC
examplesJson.explanation: Queued commands return QUEUED, not their result. EXEC executes them in order and returns one array element per command, so results arrive together at the end.

examplesJson.input: MULTI then SET account:log "started" then INCR account:owner then SET account:log "finished" then EXEC then GET account:log
examplesJson.output:
OK
QUEUED
QUEUED
QUEUED
1) OK
2) (error) ERR value is not an integer or out of range
3) OK
"finished"
examplesJson.explanation: INCR on the string "ann" fails at execution time, but the SET before it stays applied and the SET after it still runs — Redis has no rollback.

examplesJson.input: MULTI then SET account:tmp 1 then NOSUCHCOMMAND arg then EXEC then EXISTS account:tmp
examplesJson.output:
OK
QUEUED
(error) ERR unknown command 'NOSUCHCOMMAND', with args beginning with: 'arg' 
(error) EXECABORT Transaction discarded because of previous errors.
(integer) 0
examplesJson.explanation: An error Redis can detect while queueing poisons the transaction, so EXEC refuses to run anything at all and account:tmp is never created.

hintsJson: Opening the block changes what every following command replies — look at the reply, not at the value you expected.
MULTI starts queuing, EXEC runs the queue and returns one reply per command, DISCARD throws the queue away.
Errors come in two kinds: rejected while queueing (wrong command name or wrong arity) and raised while executing (right command, wrong data type).
A queue-time error makes EXEC return EXECABORT and run nothing; a runtime error is reported as one element of the EXEC array while every other command still takes effect.

starterCodeJson.name: commands.redis
starterCodeJson.code:
SET account:balance 100
SET account:owner "ann"
____
INCRBY account:balance 50
GET account:balance
____
GET account:balance
____
INCRBY account:balance 10
____
GET account:balance
____
SET account:log "started"
INCR account:owner
SET account:log "finished"
____
GET account:log
GET account:owner
____
SET account:tmp 1
NOSUCHCOMMAND arg
____
EXISTS account:tmp

solutionCodeJson.name: commands.redis
solutionCodeJson.code:
SET account:balance 100
SET account:owner "ann"
MULTI
INCRBY account:balance 50
GET account:balance
EXEC
GET account:balance
MULTI
INCRBY account:balance 10
DISCARD
GET account:balance
MULTI
SET account:log "started"
INCR account:owner
SET account:log "finished"
EXEC
GET account:log
GET account:owner
MULTI
SET account:tmp 1
NOSUCHCOMMAND arg
EXEC
EXISTS account:tmp

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
MULTI switches the connection into a queuing mode: from that point every command is parsed, checked for a valid name and arity, and stored, replying QUEUED rather than executing. EXEC then runs the whole queue as a single unit against the single-threaded server, so no other client's command can interleave, and returns an array with one element per queued command in order. That is the real guarantee, and it is genuinely useful — a "read the balance and write the derived total" pair cannot see a value that changed halfway through. DISCARD exits queuing mode and drops the queue, which is what you call when your application logic decides mid-way that the write should not happen.

The essential lesson is the third block. Redis distinguishes two error classes. A queue-time error — an unknown command, or the wrong number of arguments — is detected while the command is being queued; Redis flags the transaction and EXEC answers EXECABORT, running nothing, which is why account:tmp never exists. A runtime error such as INCR against the string "ann" cannot be detected without looking at the data, so the command queues happily and fails only during EXEC. At that moment Redis does exactly what its docs say: it records the error as one element of the reply array and carries on with the remaining commands. The proof is that account:log ends at "finished", written by the command after the failure, while the "started" written before it was never undone. There is no rollback, no savepoint, and no way to ask for one.

Two practical consequences follow. First, inspect every element of the EXEC reply. Many clients surface only "the transaction executed" and hide per-command errors inside the array, so a partially applied transaction can run in production for months unnoticed. Second, prevent runtime errors rather than handling them, since they are almost always type mistakes — incrementing a key that holds text, pushing to a key that holds a hash — which means disciplined key naming and type conventions do more for correctness here than any error handler. When you genuinely need conditional logic ("only decrement if the value is above zero"), MULTI cannot express it because the queued commands cannot see each other's results; the answer is either the optimistic-locking WATCH pattern in the next exercise or a Lua script, which is a later module. Note finally that EXEC and DISCARD both end the transaction, so the connection returns to normal mode either way.
exercises.diagramMermaid:
flowchart TD
  A[MULTI] --> B[Commands reply QUEUED]
  B --> C{Error while queueing}
  C -->|Yes such as unknown command| D[EXEC returns EXECABORT and nothing runs]
  C -->|No| E[EXEC runs every queued command in order]
  E --> F{Runtime error such as INCR on text}
  F -->|Yes| G[Error appears as one array element and all other commands still apply]
  F -->|No| H[Array of successful replies]

exercises.title: Watch Encodings Change with OBJECT ENCODING as Collections Grow
concepts: int and embstr and raw strings
exercises.problemHtml:
Every Redis type has more than one internal representation, and the one in use decides both memory cost and command complexity. Small collections are stored as a listpack — a single flat, contiguous blob with no per-element pointers, which is compact and cache-friendly but has O(N) lookup. Once a collection outgrows a configured threshold, Redis promotes it to a real hash table or skip list: more memory per element, but O(1) or O(log N) access. OBJECT ENCODING shows which one a key is using right now, and knowing this explains most "why is Redis using so much memory" investigations.

Using redis-cli, make each transition visible:

  • Strings. SET n:counter 42 is stored as int, an actual integer rather than text. SET s:short "hello" is embstr, a short immutable string allocated together with its object header. Then APPEND s:short " world" and check again: the encoding is now raw, because appending needs a separately allocated, resizable buffer.
  • Hash promotion. HSET profile:1 name "Ann" city "Hanoi" gives listpack. Add a single field whose value is 72 characters long — the scaffold provides it — and the encoding flips to hashtable, because the default hash-max-listpack-value is 64 bytes.
  • Promotion is one-way. HDEL profile:1 bio puts the hash back to two small fields, yet OBJECT ENCODING still reports hashtable. Redis never demotes.
  • Sets have three encodings. SADD ids:num 1 2 3 gives intset, a sorted array of integers — the most compact form there is. Add the non-numeric member "four" and it becomes listpack, because an intset can hold integers only.
  • The others. ZADD board 1 "alice" 2 "bob" and RPUSH queue:jobs "a" "b" are both listpack while small; a sorted set promotes to skiplist and a list to quicklist.
  • Read the thresholds. CONFIG GET hash-max-listpack-entries returns 512 and CONFIG GET hash-max-listpack-value returns 64 — the two limits that triggered the promotion above.

The scaffold contains the long value and leaves the commands and inspected keys blank.
exercises.inputSpec: A clean Redis database. Keys of five different types — n:counter, s:short, profile:1, ids:num, board, queue:jobs — are created small and then pushed past an encoding threshold.
exercises.outputSpec: OBJECT ENCODING returns "int" for n:counter, "embstr" for s:short and "raw" after APPEND (which returns (integer) 11); profile:1 is "listpack" with two fields and "hashtable" after the 72-character bio is added, and still "hashtable" after HDEL removes it with HLEN back to 2; ids:num is "intset" and becomes "listpack" once "four" is added; board and queue:jobs are both "listpack"; CONFIG GET returns hash-max-listpack-entries 512 and hash-max-listpack-value 64.
exercises.constraints: Do not change any CONFIG value — only read them. Do not use DEBUG commands; they are disabled on this server. The bio value must be longer than 64 bytes to force the promotion. Never write code that depends on a particular encoding; treat OBJECT ENCODING as a diagnostic, not an API.

examplesJson.input: SET s:short "hello" then OBJECT ENCODING s:short then APPEND s:short " world" then OBJECT ENCODING s:short
examplesJson.explanation: A short string is embedded in the same allocation as its object header; APPEND needs a growable buffer, so Redis converts it to the raw representation.

examplesJson.input: HSET profile:1 name "Ann" city "Hanoi" then OBJECT ENCODING profile:1 then HSET profile:1 bio with a 72-character value then OBJECT ENCODING profile:1
examplesJson.explanation: Two short fields fit the compact listpack. One value longer than hash-max-listpack-value of 64 bytes promotes the whole hash to a real hash table.

examplesJson.input: SADD ids:num 1 2 3 then OBJECT ENCODING ids:num then SADD ids:num "four" then OBJECT ENCODING ids:num
examplesJson.explanation: A set of only integers uses the specialised intset encoding; a single non-integer member makes that impossible, so the set converts to a listpack.

hintsJson: One command reports the internal representation of any key — run it before and after every mutation to see what changed.
OBJECT ENCODING key returns a name such as int, embstr, raw, listpack, intset, hashtable, skiplist or quicklist.
A collection stays in its compact listpack form until it exceeds a configured limit on either the number of entries or the size of a single element.

CONFIG GET hash-max-listpack-entries and CONFIG GET hash-max-listpack-value show the two thresholds, defaulting to 512 and 64; crossing either one promotes the hash permanently, and removing the offending field does not demote it.

solutionCodeJson.name: commands.redis
solutionCodeJson.code:
SET n:counter 42
OBJECT ENCODING n:counter
SET s:short "hello"
OBJECT ENCODING s:short
APPEND s:short " world"
OBJECT ENCODING s:short
HSET profile:1 name "Ann" city "Hanoi"
OBJECT ENCODING profile:1
HSET profile:1 bio "aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa"
OBJECT ENCODING profile:1
HDEL profile:1 bio
HLEN profile:1
OBJECT ENCODING profile:1

OBJECT ENCODING ids:num
SADD ids:num "four"
OBJECT ENCODING ids:num

CONFIG GET hash-max-listpack-entries
CONFIG GET hash-max-listpack-value

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
Redis separates the type a key exposes from the encoding it uses underneath. TYPE profile:1 says hash both before and after the promotion in this exercise; only OBJECT ENCODING reveals that the storage changed from a listpack to a hash table. A listpack is one contiguous allocation holding every element back to back with no pointers and no per-element overhead, which is why a small hash can cost a tenth of what the equivalent hash table costs; the trade is that finding a field means scanning the blob, so it is only used while the collection is small enough that O(N) over a handful of entries beats a pointer chase. Cross a threshold and Redis rebuilds the object into a real hash table (or a skip list for sorted sets, a quicklist for lists), paying pointers and allocator overhead per element in exchange for O(1) or O(log N) access.

The promotion rules for hashes are the two configs read at the end: more than 512 entries, or any single element longer than 64 bytes, and the hash is converted. Sets and sorted sets have the analogous set-max-listpack-* and zset-max-listpack-* settings, and sets get a third, even tighter option — intset, a plain sorted array of integers with no per-member overhead at all, which is why SADD ids:num 1 2 3 reports intset and why adding the single member "four" forces a conversion. On the string side, int means the value is held as a native long rather than as text, and embstr means a short string allocated in the same block as its object header, saving one allocation. APPEND destroys that optimisation because an embedded string cannot grow, so the key becomes raw.

The behaviour to remember above all is that conversion is one-way. After HDEL profile:1 bio the hash is back to two tiny fields, yet the encoding is still hashtable — Redis never scans for demotion opportunities, because doing so on every delete would be expensive and would thrash on workloads that hover at the boundary. The practical consequence is real: one oversized field written once, perhaps by a debug script, permanently inflates the memory of that key, and repeated across millions of keys that is a multi-gigabyte regression with no visible cause. If you must recover the compact form, rewrite the key from scratch. Two closing cautions: never branch application logic on an encoding name, since these are implementation details that have already changed once — ziplist became listpack in Redis 7 — and resist raising the thresholds to "save memory", because doing so also lengthens the O(N) scans and can quietly turn a fast command into a slow one on the single-threaded server. Pair this command with OBJECT FREQ, MEMORY USAGE key, and INFO memory when you are actually chasing a memory problem.

exercises.title: Implement Optimistic Locking with WATCH and Compare It to Pipelining
exercises.problemHtml:
MULTI alone cannot express "decrement the stock only if it is still what I read", because queued commands cannot see each other's replies. WATCH supplies the missing piece: it marks keys, and if any watched key is modified between the WATCH and the EXEC, the EXEC does nothing and returns (nil). That is optimistic locking — no lock is taken, the conflict is merely detected, and the caller retries. It is the correct pattern for the read-decide-write cycle behind stock reservation, wallet debits, and seat booking.

Using redis-cli on a single connection, produce all three outcomes. A watched key modified by your own connection aborts the transaction just as another client's write would, which is what makes this reproducible in one session:

  • Seed SET stock:sku1 5.
  • Success. WATCH stock:sku1, GET stock:sku1 (the read your decision is based on — "5"), then MULTI, DECRBY stock:sku1 2, EXEC. Nothing touched the key, so EXEC returns the array 1) (integer) 3. Confirm with GET.
  • Conflict. WATCH stock:sku1, then SET stock:sku1 99 to simulate a competing writer, then MULTI, DECRBY stock:sku1 2, EXEC. This time EXEC returns (nil) and the decrement never happened — GET is "99", not 97.
  • Abandon. WATCH stock:sku1, MULTI, DECRBY stock:sku1 9, then DISCARD. The queue is dropped and the watch is cleared too; GET is still "99".
  • Retry. Watch again, queue the same DECRBY stock:sku1 9, and EXEC — it now succeeds, returning 1) (integer) 90. This is the retry step a real client loops on.
  • Finish with UNWATCH, which clears any remaining watches without running anything.

The scaffold gives the four blocks with the transaction keywords blank.
exercises.inputSpec: A clean Redis database and a single client connection. One key, stock:sku1, is seeded with 5 and then modified by four WATCH/MULTI blocks.
exercises.outputSpec: WATCH replies OK; the first GET returns "5"; the first EXEC returns an array with (integer) 3 and GET then returns "3"; in the conflict block the interfering SET replies OK, EXEC returns (nil) and GET returns "99"; in the DISCARD block DISCARD replies OK and GET still returns "99"; the retry EXEC returns an array with (integer) 90 and GET returns "90"; UNWATCH replies OK.
exercises.constraints: Everything must run on one connection. Do not use SETNX-style locks, Lua, or a client-side sleep-and-retry — the mechanism under test is WATCH. Do not read the value inside MULTI; the decision read must happen after WATCH and before MULTI. A nil EXEC means "retry", never "error".

examplesJson.input: SET stock:sku1 5 then WATCH stock:sku1 then GET stock:sku1 then MULTI then DECRBY stock:sku1 2 then EXEC then GET stock:sku1
examplesJson.explanation: No one modified the watched key between WATCH and EXEC, so the transaction executes normally and returns the reply array.

examplesJson.input: WATCH stock:sku1 then SET stock:sku1 99 then MULTI then DECRBY stock:sku1 2 then EXEC then GET stock:sku1
examplesJson.explanation: The watched key changed after WATCH, so EXEC aborts and returns nil. The DECRBY never ran, which is why the value is 99 rather than 97.

examplesJson.input: WATCH stock:sku1 then MULTI then DECRBY stock:sku1 9 then EXEC then GET stock:sku1
examplesJson.explanation: The retry after a conflict: watch again, re-queue the write, and this time nothing intervened so EXEC applies the decrement.

hintsJson: You cannot branch inside a transaction, so instead of preventing a conflict you detect one and start over.
WATCH key [key ...] marks keys as of that moment; the following EXEC runs only if none of them changed.
Do the decision read after WATCH but before MULTI, so the value you based the decision on is the value being guarded.
An aborted EXEC returns (nil) rather than an error — the client must treat nil as "retry the whole block from WATCH". EXEC, DISCARD and UNWATCH all clear the watch list.

GET stock:sku1
MULTI

____
GET stock:sku1
____ stock:sku1

MULTI

solutionCodeJson.name: commands.redis
solutionCodeJson.code:
SET stock:sku1 5
WATCH stock:sku1
GET stock:sku1
MULTI
DECRBY stock:sku1 2
EXEC
GET stock:sku1
WATCH stock:sku1
SET stock:sku1 99
MULTI
DECRBY stock:sku1 2
EXEC
GET stock:sku1
WATCH stock:sku1
MULTI
DECRBY stock:sku1 9
DISCARD
GET stock:sku1
WATCH stock:sku1
MULTI
DECRBY stock:sku1 9
EXEC
GET stock:sku1
UNWATCH

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
WATCH attaches a dirty flag to each named key on the current connection. Any write to a watched key — by another client, or as this exercise shows by the same connection — sets that flag, and EXEC checks it before executing anything. If the flag is set, nothing runs and the reply is the RESP null, printed as (nil). That single return value is the entire contract, and it is where implementations go wrong: (nil) is not an error and must not be logged and swallowed. It means "the world moved under you", and the only correct response is to loop back to the WATCH, re-read, re-decide, and try again. The conflict block here proves the guard works: the value ends at 99, not 97, because the queued DECRBY was discarded rather than applied to data it was never computed against.

The ordering matters as much as the commands. The decision read has to sit after WATCH and before MULTI: after, so the value you read is the one being guarded, and before, because a read inside MULTI only returns QUEUED and its result arrives too late to branch on. That gives the canonical loop — watch, read, decide in the client, queue the writes, exec, and retry on nil. Because nothing is locked, there is no lock to leak if a client crashes and no possibility of deadlock, which is why this is preferable to a SET NX mutex whenever the conflict rate is low. Under heavy contention on one hot key the retries themselves become the bottleneck, and a Lua script — which runs the read and the decision server-side in one pass — is the better tool. Note that EXEC, DISCARD and UNWATCH all clear the watch list, so the DISCARD block above ends fully unwatched, and forgetting that a plain EXEC resets watches is a classic source of "my retry loop watched nothing".

Finally, the distinction the module title implies: pipelining is not a transaction. Pipelining means writing several commands to the socket without waiting for each reply, so ten commands cost one round trip instead of ten — a pure latency optimisation, with no isolation whatsoever, since other clients' commands may interleave freely with yours. MULTI/EXEC gives isolation, since the queued block runs with nothing interleaved, but by itself gives no round-trip saving unless the client also pipelines the queuing. The two compose: most client libraries send MULTI, the queued commands, and EXEC in one pipelined write, getting both benefits. Choose by intent — pipeline when you have many independent commands and only want fewer round trips, use MULTI/EXEC when the commands must not be interleaved, and add WATCH only when the writes depend on a value you read first.
exercises.diagramMermaid:
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client
    participant R as Redis
    C->>R: WATCH stock sku1
    R-->>C: OK
    C->>R: GET stock sku1
    R-->>C: value five
    Note over C: Decide in the client
    C->>R: MULTI then DECRBY then EXEC
    alt Watched key unchanged
        R-->>C: Array of replies and the write is applied
    else Watched key modified by anyone
        R-->>C: nil so retry from WATCH
    end

exercises.title: Design a Store Analytics Keyspace by Matching Each Requirement to a Type
exercises.problemHtml:
Every structure in this module exists because a different requirement makes it the cheapest correct answer. This capstone gives you five requirements at once for a retail analytics dashboard, and the work is choosing the type before typing the command. Build the whole keyspace for 2026-07-20 in one session.

The requirements, and what each one forces:

  • "How many of our registered members visited today, and was member 8 one of them?" Members have dense numeric ids and the answer must be exact and per-member queryable — that is a bitmap. Mark members 3, 8 and 15 on dau:2026-07-20, then report BITCOUNT, which is 3.
  • "Roughly how many distinct anonymous sessions did we see?" Session tokens are opaque strings, the volume is huge, and an estimate is acceptable — that is a HyperLogLog. PFADD visitors:2026-07-20 with "sess-a" "sess-b" "sess-c" "sess-a" and report PFCOUNT, which is 3.
  • "Which stores are within 3 km of the customer?" Proximity ranking over coordinates — that is a geo key. GEOADD stores with 105.8542 21.0285 "store-central", 105.8350 21.0378 "store-west", and 106.6822 20.9500 "store-coast", then GEOSEARCH stores FROMLONLAT 105.8500 21.0300 BYRADIUS 3 km WITHDIST ASC. Two stores qualify. Also measure GEODIST stores "store-central" "store-coast" km.
  • "Keep an immutable, replayable record of every checkout." Append-only, multiple independent readers, no destructive reads — that is a stream. XADD events:2026-07-20 twice with explicit ids 1700000000000-1 (kind checkout, store store-central, total 42) and 1700000000000-2 (kind checkout, store store-west, total 17). Check XLEN, then read the first entry with XRANGE events:2026-07-20 - + COUNT 1.
  • "Publish revenue and the daily report together, with no reader ever seeing one updated without the other." That is isolation, not durability — MULTI/EXEC. In one transaction, INCRBY revenue:2026-07-20 59 (the two checkout totals) and HSET report:2026-07-20 dau 3 stores 3 events 2. Then read both back.
  • Finish by inspecting what you built: OBJECT ENCODING report:2026-07-20 is listpack, OBJECT ENCODING dau:2026-07-20 is raw — a bitmap really is a string — and TYPE events:2026-07-20 is stream.

The scaffold gives every requirement as a blank command line. Choose the command, then fill it in.
exercises.inputSpec: A clean Redis database. Six keys are built for the day 2026-07-20: the bitmap dau:2026-07-20, the HyperLogLog visitors:2026-07-20, the geo key stores, the stream events:2026-07-20, the counter revenue:2026-07-20 and the hash report:2026-07-20.
exercises.outputSpec: The three SETBIT calls each return (integer) 0 and BITCOUNT returns 3; PFADD returns (integer) 1 and PFCOUNT returns 3; GEOADD returns (integer) 3 and GEOSEARCH returns store-central at "0.4671" km then store-west at "1.7828" km, with GEODIST central to coast "86.4265"; the two XADD calls echo their ids, XLEN returns 2 and XRANGE COUNT 1 returns the 1700000000000-1 entry; inside the transaction both commands reply QUEUED and EXEC returns (integer) 59 then (integer) 3; GET revenue:2026-07-20 returns "59" and HGETALL report:2026-07-20 returns dau 3, stores 3, events 2; OBJECT ENCODING returns "listpack" for the report hash and "raw" for the bitmap, and TYPE returns stream.
exercises.constraints: Each requirement must be met by the structure named for it — do not model members as a set, sessions as a set, or the event log as a list. GEOADD takes longitude before latitude. Stream ids must be the explicit ones given so the run is reproducible. The revenue and report writes must be in one MULTI/EXEC block. Do not use Lua or consumer groups.

examplesJson.input: SETBIT dau:2026-07-20 3 1, SETBIT dau:2026-07-20 8 1, SETBIT dau:2026-07-20 15 1, BITCOUNT dau:2026-07-20, then PFADD visitors:2026-07-20 "sess-a" "sess-b" "sess-c" "sess-a" and PFCOUNT visitors:2026-07-20
examplesJson.explanation: Dense member ids go in a bitmap where BITCOUNT is exact; opaque session tokens go in a HyperLogLog where PFADD reports only that the sketch changed and the duplicate "sess-a" is absorbed.

examplesJson.input: GEOSEARCH stores FROMLONLAT 105.8500 21.0300 BYRADIUS 3 km WITHDIST ASC then GEODIST stores "store-central" "store-coast" km
examplesJson.explanation: Only two stores fall inside the 3 km circle, returned nearest first with their distances; store-coast is 86 km away, which the explicit GEODIST confirms.

examplesJson.input: MULTI then INCRBY revenue:2026-07-20 59 then HSET report:2026-07-20 dau 3 stores 3 events 2 then EXEC then GET revenue:2026-07-20
examplesJson.explanation: Both writes are queued and applied with nothing interleaved, so no reader can observe the revenue updated while the report still shows yesterday numbers.

hintsJson: Read each requirement for three signals: are the identifiers dense integers, must the answer be exact, and does anyone need to enumerate the members afterwards.

Dense integer ids plus an exact per-id answer means a bitmap; opaque ids plus an acceptable estimate means a HyperLogLog; coordinates plus ranking means a geo key; an append-only record with several independent readers means a stream.
Bitmap commands are SETBIT and BITCOUNT; the sketch is PFADD and PFCOUNT; proximity is GEOADD then GEOSEARCH FROMLONLAT with BYRADIUS, WITHDIST and ASC; the log is XADD then XLEN and XRANGE.

Wrap the two summary writes in MULTI and EXEC so they land together, then verify the internals with OBJECT ENCODING and TYPE — the report hash is a listpack, the bitmap is a raw string, and the event log has its own stream type.

exercises.solutionExplanationHtml:
The skill being trained is reading a requirement and hearing the structure it demands. Three questions decide it every time. Are the identifiers dense small integers? If yes, a bitmap addresses them directly, costs one bit each, and answers both "how many" with BITCOUNT and "was member 8 there" with GETBIT exactly. Must the answer be exact, and will anyone need the members back? If not, a HyperLogLog counts unbounded opaque session tokens in a fixed sketch — the right call precisely because nobody will ever ask it to list the sessions. Is the data coordinates ranked by proximity? Then a geo key, which is a sorted set of geohash scores, turns "within 3 km, nearest first" into one GEOSEARCH. And an immutable record that several independent consumers must replay is a stream, because reading it is non-destructive and every entry keeps a stable id — a list would force one reader to consume events the others still need.

Choosing wrongly is not a style error, it is a cost or correctness error. Modelling the member DAU as a set would work but cost tens of bytes per member instead of one bit, times one key per day. Modelling anonymous sessions as a set would be exact and would exhaust memory. Modelling the checkout log as a list would make LPOP destroy events the analytics job still needs, and index-based paging would break the moment the list is trimmed. Note the deliberate asymmetry between the two counting structures in the same dashboard: exactness is demanded where a human can dispute the number, and traded away where only a trend matters.

The MULTI/EXEC block does one specific job here — isolation, so no dashboard read can catch revenue:2026-07-20 already updated while report:2026-07-20 still holds the previous values. It is worth being precise about what it does not do, per the earlier exercise: there is no rollback, so if revenue:2026-07-20 had held a non-numeric value the INCRBY would have failed inside the reply array while the HSET still applied. Since these writes derive from values read beforehand, a production version would wrap them in WATCH on the source keys and retry when EXEC returns nil.

The closing inspection ties the module together. OBJECT ENCODING dau:2026-07-20 answering raw confirms that a bitmap is genuinely a string with bit-addressing commands layered on top — no separate type exists — just as a geo key reports zset and a HyperLogLog reports string. Only the stream has a type of its own. The report hash sits in the compact listpack encoding because it is small, and would flip permanently to hashtable if any field value exceeded 64 bytes. One thing this keyspace still lacks is lifetime management: every key here is dated, so a real deployment would attach a TTL at creation and let old days expire rather than accumulate — the key-management discipline from module 424 applied to the structures from this one.
exercises.diagramMermaid:
flowchart TD
  R[Requirement] --> A[Dense integer ids and an exact answer uses a bitmap with SETBIT and BITCOUNT]

  R --> D[Append only replayable record for many readers uses a stream with XADD and XRANGE]
  R --> E[Two writes that must land together uses MULTI and EXEC]
